comments.text: I do not know how to solve the problem completely, but there is a simple improvement $ f(n)\gg {\log n\over \log\log n} $. Let $F(n,m)$ denote the least integer $L$ such that the interval $(m,m+L]$ contains distinct multiples of $1,2,\dots,n$. For the set $A=\{2,\dots,n\}$, the defining property of $f(n)$ implies $F(n,m)\le f(n)n$ for every $m$. On the other hand, van Doorn proved that for all sufficiently large $n$ there exists $m=m(n)$ such that $ F(n,m)\gg n{\log n\over \log\log n}.$ Combining the two inequalities gives the claim. It is natural to guess that the truth is much closer to the lower bound than to the upper bound. Disclosure statement :Based on @Prof Tao comment,I have used Chatgpt thinking 5.4 for help and for the above combination [ErPo80] P. Erdős and C. Pomerance, Matching the natural numbers up to $n$ with distinct multiples in another interval, Indag. Math. 42 (1980), 147–151. [vD26] W. van Doorn, On the length of an interval that contains distinct multiples of the first $n$ positive integers, arXiv:2601.16972, 2026.

The main points of the above remark can be summarized as follows: the lower bound of Erdős-Surányi proof (which gives $c = 1 - \frac{1 + \log \log 2}{\log 2} \approx 0.086$) can be improved to $f(n) \gg \frac{\log n}{\log \log n}$ by restricting to the special case $A = \{2, 3, \ldots, n+1\}$ and using van Doorn's bound on #711. If one instead uses the results of [ErPo80], one already obtains the intermediate lower bound $f(n) \gg \sqrt{\log n/\log\log n}$. (These observations were obtained from conversation with Wouter and GPT.) As a reminder, all use of AI tools need to be disclosed, and unnecessarily lengthy AI-generated text should be avoided in the comments to this forum; replacement with a shorter human-generated summary is strongly preferred. (I have already edited the original answer to condense it by about 75% without any significant loss of content.)
